title: Improved sample complexity bound for sample-based Lindbladian simulation
authors: Siheon Park, Youngjin Seo, Byeongseon Go, Dhrumil Patel, Mark M. Wilde, Hyukjoon Kwon
abstract: We establish improved sample-complexity bounds for sample-based Lindbladian simulation based on the Wave Matrix Lindbladization (WML) algorithm. For a jump operator $L$ with dimension $d$, we derive an explicit non-asymptotic sample complexity bound $n_d^*(t,\varepsilon) \le \left( \frac{2d+3}{8} \right) \|L\|_\infty^2 \left( \frac{t^2}{\varepsilon} \right)$, holding for simulation time $t$ and error $\varepsilon$. This refines the dimension dependence of the best previously known bound, $O(d^2 t^2/\varepsilon)$, from [Go et al., Quantum Sci. Tech. 10, 045058 (2025)]. Remarkably, we show that this dimensional overhead can be entirely avoided when $\| L\|_\infty^2 = O(1/d)$, a condition satisfied with high probability for random Lindblad operators, yielding a typical-case sample complexity of $O(t^2/\varepsilon)$. On the other hand, in the worst case, we show that WML necessarily requires $Ω(dt^2/\varepsilon)$ samples by constructing an explicit example with a rank-one Lindblad operator. Our results reveal a sharp dichotomy between typical and adversarial sample complexities in Lindbladian simulation, thereby strengthening the theoretical foundations of sample-based quantum algorithms.
summary: This paper significantly improves the theoretical bounds on the sample complexity required to simulate open quantum dynamics governed by Lindblad equations. By analyzing the WML algorithm, the authors show that while the worst-case complexity scales poorly with dimension, the typical case complexity scales optimally as $O(t^2/\varepsilon)$, which is a major theoretical advance for quantum simulation.
main_problem: The paper aims to derive and improve the sample-complexity bounds required for simulating open quantum system dynamics governed by Lindblad master equations.
main_result: It establishes an improved upper bound for the sample complexity, showing that for typical (random) Lindblad operators, the dependence on system dimension $d$ can vanish, achieving $O(t^2/\varepsilon)$.
method: The analysis uses sample-based quantum process tomography techniques, comparing the WML approximation to the true evolution using the diamond distance and leveraging random matrix theory for the typical case.
model_or_system: The system models open quantum dynamics described by the Gorini–Kossakowski–Sudarshan–Lindblad (GKSL) master equation, characterized by a Lindblad operator $L$. The simulation is performed using the Wave Matrix Lindbladization (WML) algorithm.
key_observables: Sample complexity ($n^*_d(t, \varepsilon)$), which quantifies the number of program state copies needed to approximate the evolution $e^{Lt}$ within error $\varepsilon$.
important_parameters: Simulation time ($t$), error tolerance ($\varepsilon$), system dimension ($d$), and the norm of the Lindblad operator ($\|L\|_\infty$).
main_assumptions: The typical case analysis assumes that the Lindblad operators are drawn from random distributions (e.g., Ginibre ensemble), while the worst-case analysis considers adversarial operators like rank-one Lindblad operators.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first establishing the improved upper bound, then contrasting this with the lower bound derived from specific operator constructions, and finally analyzing the sharp dichotomy between typical and adversarial sample complexities.
why_interesting: This work provides crucial theoretical guarantees on the resource scaling (sample complexity) needed for simulating realistic open quantum systems, directly impacting the feasibility assessment of quantum algorithms for dissipative processes.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
